title: Locker hook rug basics
subtitle: Drawing cut fabric strips through rug canvas with a locker hook to build a flat, durable rug surface
excerpt: A locker hook is a hook with a hollow shaft through which a locking yarn is threaded. As each loop of fabric or yarn is drawn through rug canvas, the locking thread running through the shaft catches inside the loop and prevents it pulling back. The result is a flat, firm rug surface worked row by row on a stiff canvas mesh. Locker hooking uses fabric strips cut from old clothing or yardage, making it a practical way to use textile scraps.
sourceNotes: Locker hook rug making is documented in contemporary rug-making instruction and textile crafts guides. Method is observation-led and consistent with workshop teaching practice.

content.text: draws fabric strips through rug canvas in one motion and locks each loop simultaneously via the
in its shaft. No separate securing step is needed: each strip locks as it is pulled through.

items.qty: cut to desired rug size plus 5 cm border
items.name: Rug canvas (5-count: 5 holes per 2.5 cm)

items.qty: 2-3 m per 10 x 10 cm of rug area
items.name: Fabric strips, 1-2 cm wide (woven cotton or jersey)

items.qty: 1-2 m per 10 cm row of canvas
items.name: Locking yarn (smooth cotton or linen yarn)

content.text: Step 1: Prepare the canvas and fabric strips

Bind the edges of the rug canvas with masking tape to prevent the cut edges from fraying and catching on the hook. Cut fabric strips 1-2 cm wide and 20-30 cm long: cut woven fabric on the straight grain; cut jersey fabric slightly narrower (1 cm) as it stretches. Sort strips by colour if working a design.
Step 2: Thread the locking yarn and begin

Thread the locking yarn through the eye at the top of the locker hook shaft and draw it through until you have about 10 cm of tail. Hold the canvas flat and start at one edge. Push the hook down through a canvas hole, hook a fabric strip at its midpoint, and draw the loop up through the hole. The locking yarn is carried through the loop automatically as you pull. Move one canvas hole to the right and repeat.
Step 3: Work rows across the canvas

Work left to right across each row, drawing one loop per canvas hole. Each fabric strip is centred in the loop: both halves extend above the canvas surface as a loop. When a strip runs out, simply start the next: there is no need to knot strips together. The locking yarn holds the ends in place. At the end of each row, cut the locking yarn leaving a 5 cm tail, thread a new length for the next row.

When the rug is complete, fold the border canvas (5 cm) to the reverse and stitch it down with a blunt darning needle and linen thread. This secures the edges without a visible hem on the front face. To add a non-slip backing, cut a piece of non-slip rug liner to size and attach it to the reverse with a few stitches at the corners.

items.fix: Check that the locking yarn runs through the hollow shaft and out through the eye before starting. If it breaks mid-row, re-thread from the last secured loop and continue.
items.cause: Locking yarn not properly threaded through the shaft, or locking yarn broke during working
items.problem: Loops pull back through the canvas and unravel

items.fix: For 5-count canvas, strips should be no wider than 1.5 cm. Test one strip in a hole before cutting the full batch. Jersey fabric can be wider as it compresses; woven fabric cannot.
items.cause: Strips cut too wide for the canvas count
items.problem: The fabric strips bunch and jam in the canvas holes

items.fix: Pull each loop to a consistent height above the canvas (about 1 cm for a flat surface). Work at a steady pace rather than pulling some loops fully and others partially.
items.cause: Loops drawn to different depths on different holes
items.problem: The finished rug surface is uneven with different loop heights
